content.text:
Harden the outline-decorations implementation now on `main`. This is the productionization pass for the decoration system that came out of the experiments (additive indentation + CSS-gradient guides + SVG icon block markers) — polish and robustness only, no new features. Work through the SDD loop: the authoritative work list is the remaining unchecked tasks in `openspec/changes/outline-decorations/tasks.md` (the hardening section, plus any mobile findings recorded there by the mobile-testing PR's triage) — use `/openspec-apply-change` to drive it. Branch off `main`, draft PR back to `main`, one commit per task or coherent task group, checking tasks off as they land.

**Read before touching anything**: `docs/research/11-decoration-lessons.md` — every entry is a mistake already made once in this codebase, and several directly constrain this work (never append DOM children into a `.cm-line` — confirmed 100%+ CPU feedback loop via CM6's mutation observer; measure the chevron's `.collapse-indicator.collapse-icon` glyph, not its zero-width `.cm-fold-indicator` wrapper; when a box-widening value drives multiple painted layers, compute it once and thread it everywhere; matching beats escalating in cascade fights; read native values live instead of hardcoding). Then `docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md`'s "Next steps: hardening 5a" section for each task's full rationale, and the openspec change's `design.md` for the architecture as specced.

Guidance on the known tasks (defer to tasks.md where it says more):

- **Fold-chevron live measurement**: replace the two hardcoded constants in the static CSS transform (`0.425rem` half-wrapper-width, `3px` dead space) with a live-measured value, following the pattern `MarginCompensation` already establishes for `nativeMarginBasePx` (measure one representative element per render, apply uniformly). Keep the behavior the experiments validated: the reach applies to headings unconditionally (the chevron gutter is reserved even before a heading has children — markers must not jump sideways when a first child is added).
- **`app.workspace.updateOptions()` vs. the forceRedraw hack**: evaluate before replacing. The bar: after changing `markerVisibility` on a note containing *only* widget-replaced atoms (a lone table — the exact case where decoration output is byte-identical and CM6 never re-fires `docViewUpdate`), the marker updates without a mode toggle. If `updateOptions()` demonstrably passes that scenario, replace the off/on toggle; if not, document why and keep the hack. Either way the table-only scenario keeps a regression test.
- **Parse consolidation**: three ViewPlugins currently each run `parse()`/`decorate()` per transaction. Consolidate into one shared computation feeding all consumers — without breaking the `isNestedEditor` gating, the StateField/ViewPlugin split for widget atoms, or the plain-line vs. widget marker mechanisms. This is a refactor of working, heavily-tested code: the full suite is the safety net, and no behavior change is expected or acceptable.
- **Declarative settings API**: adopt `getSettingDefinitions()` on the settings tab (the one remaining lint warning; Obsidian 1.13+ settings-search support). Consult current Obsidian API docs/typings rather than guessing the shape.
- **Accessibility/polish**: `aria-hidden="true"` on marker SVGs. Then verification passes (not necessarily fixes) for RTL text, IME composition at line start, and 2–3 community themes: record findings as new tasks or a research-doc note, fixing only what's cheap and safe — same feedback-loop framing as mobile.
- **Explicitly out of scope**: the "shrinking only our own added list margin" open question stays undecided — it's a design decision to surface, not implement. If any task turns out to require a genuine design choice, stop and surface it rather than deciding unilaterally.

**Verification bar — the project's own hard rules, not suggestions**: green tests alone prove nothing about rendering. For every visual-surface change: `npm run build:plugin` (plain `npm run build` only type-checks and has produced false verification twice), screenshot the full fixture corpus (`ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES`) in both bundled themes, and run the full desktop e2e suite plus `npm run test:e2e:mobile` (mobile is a feasibility feedback loop, not a gate — triage failures per the established buckets: test artifact → fix here; real mobile rendering issue → record as task; architectural blocker → research-doc addendum). Pixel/computed-style assertions over DOM-attribute checks. Lint must stay at zero errors and must not regress the marker code. A real-vault pass in a live Obsidian instance remains the final bar for anything visual — flag explicitly in the PR description which changes need that manual pass, since it can't be automated.

Any doc/spec text you touch: neutral team voice (we/our, "manual review"/"a tester"; never you/your or session-log phrasing). Update the corresponding checklist item statuses in `docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md` as tasks land. If every hardening task completes, do NOT archive the openspec change or run sync-specs — note readiness in the PR description and leave that as a deliberate follow-up decision.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/laughedelic/Code/obsidian-true-outliner/.claude/skills/openspec-apply-change

Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change.

**Store selection:** If the user names a store (a store is a standalone OpenSpec repo registered on this machine) or the work lives in one, run `openspec store list --json` to discover registered store ids, then pass `--store <id>` on the commands that read or write specs and changes (`new change`, `status`, `instructions`, `list`, `show`, `validate`, `archive`, `doctor`, `context`). Other commands do not take the flag. Hints printed by commands already carry the flag; keep it on follow-ups. Without a store, commands act on the nearest local `openspec/` root.

**Input**: Optionally specify a change name. If omitted, check if it can be inferred from conversation context. If vague or ambiguous you MUST prompt for available changes.

**Steps**

1. **Select the change**

   If a name is provided, use it. Otherwise:
   - Infer from conversation context if the user mentioned a change
   - Auto-select if only one active change exists
   - If ambiguous, run `openspec list --json` to get available changes and use the **AskUserQuestion tool** to let the user select

   Always announce: "Using change: <name>" and how to override (e.g., `/opsx:apply <other>`).

2. **Check status to understand the schema**
   ```bash
   openspec status --change "<name>" --json
   ```
   Parse the JSON to understand:
   - `schemaName`: The workflow being used (e.g., "spec-driven")
   - `planningHome`, `changeRoot`, and `actionContext`: planning scope and edit constraints
   - Which artifact contains the tasks (typically "tasks" for spec-driven, check status for others)

3. **Get apply instructions**

   ```bash
   openspec instructions apply --change "<name>" --json
   ```

   This returns:
   - `contextFiles`: artifact ID -> array of concrete file paths (varies by schema - could be proposal/specs/design/tasks or spec/tests/implementation/docs)
   - Progress (total, complete, remaining)
   - Task list with status
   - Dynamic instruction based on current state

   **Handle states:**
   - If `state: "blocked"` (missing artifacts): show message, suggest using openspec-continue-change
   - If `state: "all_done"`: congratulate, suggest archive
   - Otherwise: proceed to implementation

4. **Read context files**

   Read every file path listed under `contextFiles` from the apply instructions output.
   The files depend on the schema being used:
   - **spec-driven**: proposal, specs, design, tasks
   - Other schemas: follow the contextFiles from CLI output

5. **Show current progress**

   Display:
   - Schema being used
   - Progress: "N/M tasks complete"
   - Remaining tasks overview
   - Dynamic instruction from CLI

6. **Implement tasks (loop until done or blocked)**

   For each pending task:
   - Show which task is being worked on
   - Make the code changes required
   - Keep changes minimal and focused
   - Mark task complete in the tasks file: `- [ ]` → `- [x]`
   - Continue to next task

   **Pause if:**
   - Task is unclear → ask for clarification
   - Implementation reveals a design issue → suggest updating artifacts
   - Error or blocker encountered → report and wait for guidance
   - User interrupts

7. **On completion or pause, show status**

   Display:
   - Tasks completed this session
   - Overall progress: "N/M tasks complete"
   - If all done: suggest archive
   - If paused: explain why and wait for guidance

**Output During Implementation**

```
## Implementing: <change-name> (schema: <schema-name>)

Working on task 3/7: <task description>
[...implementation happening...]
✓ Task complete

Working on task 4/7: <task description>
[...implementation happening...]
✓ Task complete
```

**Output On Completion**

```
## Implementation Complete

**Change:** <change-name>
**Schema:** <schema-name>
**Progress:** 7/7 tasks complete ✓

### Completed This Session
- [x] Task 1
- [x] Task 2
...

All tasks complete! Ready to archive this change.
```

**Output On Pause (Issue Encountered)**

```
## Implementation Paused

**Change:** <change-name>
**Schema:** <schema-name>
**Progress:** 4/7 tasks complete

### Issue Encountered
<description of the issue>

**Options:**
1. <option 1>
2. <option 2>
3. Other approach

What would you like to do?
```

**Guardrails**
- Keep going through tasks until done or blocked
- Always read context files before starting (from the apply instructions output)
- If task is ambiguous, pause and ask before implementing
- If implementation reveals issues, pause and suggest artifact updates
- Keep code changes minimal and scoped to each task
- Update task checkbox immediately after completing each task
- Pause on errors, blockers, or unclear requirements - don't guess
- Use contextFiles from CLI output, don't assume specific file names

**Fluid Workflow Integration**

This skill supports the "actions on a change" model:

- **Can be invoked anytime**: Before all artifacts are done (if tasks exist), after partial implementation, interleaved with other actions
- **Allows artifact updates**: If implementation reveals design issues, suggest updating artifacts - not phase-locked, work fluidly

1	# Decoration lessons: cross-experiment findings
2	
3	The accumulated non-obvious findings from the whole decoration-experiments series
4	(Experiments [1](08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md),
5	[2a/2b](09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md), and [5a/5b](10-experiment-5-block-markers.md)) —
6	carried forward the same way the original postmortem's own "carried-forward technical
7	findings" section was meant to be used
8	([06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md), which
9	remains its own separate reference for the pre-experiment failure). **Read this before
10	building anything that touches decorations, CM6 extensions, or Obsidian's native
11	rendering** — every entry below is a mistake that was actually made (often shipped) at
12	least once in this project, or a hard-won capability that a plausible mental model says
13	shouldn't exist.
14	
15	Each entry keeps its full original wording from the experiment that produced it; they are
16	grouped by theme here, not by chronology. The per-experiment docs record *which* bug each
17	finding came from.
18	
19	## CSS: cascade, box model, and painting
20	
21	- **Matching beats escalating.** When Obsidian's own CSS wins a specificity fight even
22	  against `!important`, the fix is matching its ancestor-chain specificity, not adding more
23	  `!important` (there's nowhere further to escalate past equal importance — the tie-break is
24	  specificity, then source order).
25	- **An inline style with `!important`, set via JS, always wins over any stylesheet rule**
26	  regardless of that rule's own specificity — a reliable escape hatch specifically for
27	  native-widget cascade fights that a CSS-only rule can't win.
28	- **Padding's visibility depends on where the background lives.** Padding never moves an
29	  element's own box (carried forward from the original postmortem), but whether that's
30	  visually invisible or visually offsetting depends on whether the element's own background
31	  fills the padded box (invisible, as for callout) or the visible content is nested one level
32	  further in without its own covering background (offsetting, as for a `<table>` inside a
33	  padded wrapper).
34	- **A `::before` pseudo-element's own box does NOT have to match its containing block's
35	  dimensions — `left`/`right` can widen it arbitrarily, including leftward past where a
36	  `margin`-shifted line's own box starts.** A first pass on 2b wrongly concluded the opposite
37	  ("a per-line background can only ever paint within that line's own box, full stop") and
38	  shipped it as a "confirmed structural limitation" — this was corrected only after review
39	  explicitly refused to accept that framing and asked for verification, not because the
40	  original box-model reasoning was re-examined unprompted. The actual fix: widen the pseudo by
41	  exactly the line's own known `margin-left` value (`--to-own-shift`), which brings its box's
42	  *left edge* back to the global column a shallower ancestor needs, and CSS raises no
43	  objection to a positioned descendant's box extending outside its containing block's own
44	  edges (only `overflow`/`contain` on an ancestor can clip that, and by default they don't).
45	  Confirmed live via a bisection test (a probe `::before` visible at `left: 0`, progressively
46	  clipped as `left` went more negative) that this is real, not a fluke. Generalizes beyond
47	  guide lines: any future per-line decoration that needs to reach a coordinate outside its own
48	  (possibly `margin`-shifted) line box can use the same widened-box technique instead of
49	  assuming it needs a measured overlay.
50	- **`contain: paint` (or `contain: strict`/`content`, which includes it) silently clips
51	  descendant painting to an element's own box regardless of `overflow` — a distinct mechanism
52	  from the `overflow` property, easy to miss when auditing "what could be clipping this."**
53	  Obsidian's own `app.css` sets it (`!important`, on a `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]`
54	  selector) on all four widget-replaced atom kinds (table/callout/hr/html), likely a rendering/
55	  virtualization isolation hint. It was found only by checking `getComputedStyle(...).contain`
56	  directly on the clipped element itself — `overflow` alone reported `visible` the whole time,
57	  which is what made the clipping look inexplicable at first. Overriding it needs the same
58	  "match Obsidian's own specificity, don't just add `!important`" fix Experiment 1 already
59	  established for `margin`/`padding` — a *different* property, same lesson, confirmed by
60	  checking which stylesheet rule actually won via `document.styleSheets` inspection rather than
61	  guessing from the rendered result.
62	- **Overriding a native CSS property should be checked for a legitimate reason before assuming
63	  it's safe to fight — but "it has a legitimate reason" isn't automatically "so leave it
64	  alone," it can also mean "so find where else that reason's actual mechanism lives."**
65	  Table's `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll on genuinely wide tables) sits on the same
66	  element a guide's `contain`/`overflow` overrides target, and naively forcing
67	  `overflow: visible` there does trade away that real, functional native behavior for a
68	  cosmetic guide line — confirmed live, not just theorized (a 15-column table lost its
69	  scrollbar entirely, `scrollLeft` became inert, and the *whole document* became horizontally
70	  scrollable instead of just the table). The productive next question wasn't "so don't do
71	  this," it was "why does this one property change break something else, and can that
72	  something else live on a different element?" It can: Obsidian's table widget already wraps
73	  the actual `<table>` in its own inner `.table-wrapper` div, distinct from the outer element
74	  carrying the `contain`/`overflow` fight. Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto that inner wrapper
75	  instead of the outer decouples "let our pseudo-element bleed past this box's edge" from "clip
76	  the actual wide content that needs to scroll" — two different concerns that don't have to
77	  live on the same box just because they happened to start out on the same native element.
78	  Generalizes: when overriding a native property seems to force a real trade-off, check whether
79	  the *conflicting* responsibility can be relocated to a different element in the existing DOM
80	  before accepting the trade-off as permanent.
81	- **`background-position`'s `%` component already resolves relative to (box size − image
82	  size) — it does NOT need a manual `- HALF`-style correction to "center" something.**
83	  `background-position: 50% 50%` alone centers an image; subtracting half the image's own
84	  size on top of that shifts it further than intended. This is an easy mistake to make by
85	  analogy with *length*-based positioning (where a literal offset does need that
86	  correction, since a length value places the image's top-left corner directly, with no
87	  automatic size-aware adjustment) — the two positioning modes have different semantics and
88	  don't compose the way intuition suggests. Caught only because manual review reported a marker
89	  sitting suspiciously close to a line's top edge on real content; a synthetic single-line
90	  fixture at the exact right size could easily have looked "close enough" to pass a casual
91	  screenshot glance.
92	- **When a box's own position is widened to reach a coordinate outside itself (2b's own
93	  `--to-own-shift` technique above), EVERY layer painted on that box — and the widening
94	  amount itself — must be driven from ONE shared value, computed ONCE.** This is the single
95	  most-repeated bug shape across Experiment 5b, showing up in three structurally different
96	  guises: (1) a marker's own position went unwidened while its box wasn't, so it clipped
97	  entirely at shallow depths; (2) after guides were changed to align with a marker's
98	  *center* rather than the raw depth column, the marker's own position formula kept using
99	  its OWN (narrower) shortfall while the box widened by the marker-vs-guide COMBINED one,
100	  drifting the marker off its intended column at any depth where the two values diverge;
101	  (3) after folding in the native fold-chevron's reach, a live-override code path set
102	  `--to-own-shift` to the combined `extra` alone, silently dropping the OTHER term
103	  (`ownShiftUnits * unit`) that margin-shifted lines also need there. All three are the
104	  *same* mistake at heart — two call sites that are supposed to derive from one number
105	  quietly grew independent, slightly different formulas — and all three were fixed the
106	  same way: consolidate into one function (`computeMarkerAndGuideBg`) that every caller,
107	  static and live, goes through, so there is structurally only one place left to get it
108	  wrong. Worth treating as a standing design rule for any future decoration needing
109	  box-widening, not a lesson specific to markers or guides: **when two things must move
110	  together, make it impossible to change one without the other, don't just remember to.**
111	- **A per-line "extra reach" constant is the wrong shape once a chrome adjustment applies to
112	  only SOME of the ancestors bridging that line, not all of them uniformly.** The fold-gap
113	  fix initially treated "does this line need extra reach for a fold chevron" as one
114	  per-line yes/no fact — true for a single node's own marker, but WRONG for guides, where
115	  one line can simultaneously bridge a heading-owned ancestor (needs the reach) and a
116	  paragraph-owned one (doesn't — paragraphs never fold in Obsidian's UI, even though this
117	  project's own tree lets them own children). The fix needed to track the adjustment
118	  PER ACTIVE ANCESTOR DEPTH, not per line — `computeLineGuides` grew a `headingGuideDepths`
119	  field (the subset of `guideDepths` whose owner is foldable) precisely so a downstream
120	  per-depth decision could be made correctly. A useful general question when a new
121	  chrome-clearing adjustment is added to an EXISTING per-line-aggregate mechanism (any
122	  future guide/marker refinement included): "does this adjustment apply uniformly to every
123	  contributor to this aggregate, or only some of them?" — if only some, the aggregate needs
124	  to carry per-contributor detail, not just a combined total.
125	
126	## CodeMirror 6: decorations, widgets, and coordinate systems
127	
128	- **CM6 `Decoration.line` has zero effect on Obsidian's "embed-block" replacement widgets**
129	  (tables, callouts, raw HTML, and horizontal rules) — not a partial win, not a class-merge;
130	  confirmed live that both class and inline style come back completely empty. Any atom kind
131	  Obsidian renders this way needs direct DOM patching via a `ViewPlugin`'s `docViewUpdate`
132	  hook, not a CM6 decoration. Code fences and plain blockquotes are *not* in this category —
133	  they render as genuinely plain `.cm-line`s and decorate normally.
134	- **A single broad selector plus a fact lookup by document line number is enough** to handle
135	  all four widget-replaced kinds uniformly (`.cm-embed-block, .cm-line.hr` → `posAtDOM` →
136	  `decorate()` facts by line) — no need for kind-specific branches.
137	- **CM6's coordinate systems are not interchangeable, and mixing them silently compiles.**
138	  `coordsAtPos()` is viewport-relative (like `getBoundingClientRect`); `lineBlockAt()`'s
139	  `top`/`bottom` are relative to `view.documentTop` instead — a *different* origin, even
140	  though both "sound" document-relative. Converting only one axis (x, via a `getBase()`-
141	  style subtraction) and not the other (y, left as the raw `lineBlockAt` value) type-checks
142	  fine and looks plausible in code review; it produced a ~76px vertical offset only caught
143	  by an e2e rect assertion against independently-measured `.cm-line` rects, never by the
144	  pure-function unit tests (which never touch real coordinates at all). Any experiment doing
145	  its own pixel measurement (2b's gradient positioning included, if it ends up needing any)
146	  should audit *every* axis's reference frame explicitly, not assume symmetry.
147	- **`coordsAtPos(pos, side)`'s side parameter matters at line starts, and defaults are not
148	  obviously safe.** `side: 1` ("after"-biased) at a line's very first position returns the
149	  far edge of the first character — for a list item, that's past the bullet glyph, not at
150	  its start. `side: -1` ("before"-biased) gives the character's own left edge. Untested,
151	  `side: 1` compiles and "looks right" (it did return *a* plausible-looking coordinate) —
152	  only a rect comparison against the actual rendered marker (`.cm-formatting-list`) caught
153	  the ~12px discrepancy.
154	- **A nested list item's raw source line starts with its own indentation whitespace, and
155	  Obsidian doesn't render that whitespace at literal character width.** Measuring
156	  `coordsAtPos` at a line's raw `.from` (before skipping leading whitespace) lands at
157	  wherever Obsidian's own indent mechanism collapses that whitespace to — which turned out
158	  to be the *parent* list level's own column, not the current line's marker. Any code
159	  measuring "where does this line's real content start" needs to skip
160	  `text.length - text.trimStart().length` characters first; this is general (headings/
161	  paragraphs have no leading whitespace, so it's a no-op there), not a list-item special case.
162	- **CM6 ships a first-party `layer()`/`RectangleMarker` pair (`@codemirror/view`, used
163	  internally for the selection/cursor layers) purpose-built for "measured overlay outside
164	  `.cm-content`, scrolls naturally."** `RectangleMarker` alone (just the positioned-div
165	  factory, not the full `layer()` wrapper) was reused here since `layer()`'s own `update()`
166	  hook only supports a synchronous redraw with no way to thread in the plan's requested
167	  debounce — but the full `layer()` extension is worth trying as-is for 2b or a future
168	  revisit if debouncing turns out not to matter in practice; it would remove the need for
169	  this experiment's manual scroll-coordinate calibration entirely.
170	- **A CM6 extension registered via `registerEditorExtension` applies to EVERY `EditorView`
171	  instance Obsidian creates app-wide, including nested/embedded ones a naive mental model
172	  wouldn't expect.** Obsidian renders an actively-edited table cell in Live Preview as its own
173	  separate, independent CM6 instance mounted inside the outer table widget's DOM — not a
174	  detail this project's own decoration code accounted for. A mechanism built assuming "I only
175	  run on the real top-level note" silently also runs there, and a bare line of cell text gets
176	  classified exactly like real top-level content (here: a plain paragraph, since that's the
177	  default block kind for a line with no special syntax), picking up markers/indentation meant
178	  only for the actual document and visibly corrupting the cell being edited. `editorInfoField`
179	  (Obsidian's own "which file is this editor for" field, a reliable outline-mode gate
180	  everywhere else in this project) resolves to the exact SAME object for both — confirmed live
181	  via `EditorView.findFromDOM()` on the nested instance — so state alone can never distinguish
182	  a nested editor from the real one; only DOM ancestry can (`view.dom.closest('.cm-embed-
183	  block')`), which requires `view` access a plain `StateField` doesn't have (fixed here by
184	  moving to `ViewPlugin`s). Any future CM6 extension in this project that implicitly assumes
185	  "I only run on the real note" should check this explicitly, not assume it — the leak is
186	  independently confirmed in the wild, too: obsidian-lapel's gutter renders inside per-cell
187	  editors and its changelog carries the same fix, done as a CSS hide of the symptom
188	  (`.table-cell-wrapper .cm-gutters { display: none }`) with a comment that there is no
189	  proper way to scope `registerEditorExtension` — a weaker fix than a DOM-ancestry gate,
190	  since the extension still computes, it just doesn't paint. Table cells are
191	  the one confirmed case so far, but any other Obsidian construct that edits a fragment of a
192	  document as its own nested editor would have the identical exposure.
193	
194	## Obsidian: native chrome and internals
195	
196	- **Read native values live instead of hardcoding compensations.** Subtracting a
197	  `getComputedStyle(...)`-read native padding from an own-added margin (clamped at zero)
198	  stays correct across themes/Obsidian versions; a hardcoded pixel constant would silently
199	  drift the moment a theme changes that padding.
200	- **List items already sit right of sibling text in vanilla Obsidian** — the native
201	  `text-indent`/`padding-left` hang pair reserves room for the bullet regardless of outline
202	  mode. This experiment's additive design correctly preserved that native behavior
203	  (verified: our own added margin is identical across list/paragraph/blockquote at the same
204	  depth); the extra offset users notice is pre-existing Obsidian behavior becoming more
205	  visible now that everything else aligns precisely, not a regression this experiment
206	  introduced.
207	- **A custom guide mechanism must actively coexist with Obsidian's own native "Show indent
208	  guides" setting for lists, not just avoid crashing next to it.** Real-vault side-by-side
209	  comparison (native only / both / ours only) showed native list guides are already precise
210	  (bullet to bullet) in a way a block-level, fixed-unit overlay can't match — running both
211	  at once visibly doubles lines; ours alone (if drawn for list nesting) reads as unevenly
212	  spaced. The fix wasn't CSS suppression of the native feature (obsidian-outliner's own
213	  approach, per the original postmortem) but scope reduction: never draw our guide where
214	  the *native* mechanism already owns the job (any list-item ancestor), only where native
215	  has zero representation at all (bridging a non-list ancestor into a list, or between
216	  non-list kinds). **2b will face this identical question** — a CSS-gradient guide is
217	  exactly as capable of doubling against native list guides as an overlay div is; the same
218	  list-item-ancestor exclusion should apply there too, not be treated as an 2a-specific fix.
219	- **Measure the actual visual element a fix needs to clear, not its wrapper — a wrapper can
220	  report a technically-true but practically-useless size (here, 0).** Obsidian's native fold
221	  chevron is two nested elements: `.cm-fold-indicator` (a zero-width anchor point Obsidian
222	  positions at the text's own start column) and `.collapse-indicator.collapse-icon` (the
223	  actual glyph, rendered via a negative offset extending LEFT of that anchor). Measuring the
224	  wrapper's own `getBoundingClientRect()` would have silently produced a no-op fix (0px of
225	  extra reach); the real, non-zero width was only on the child. Generalizes: when a fix
226	  needs "how much space does native UI element X occupy," verify empirically which specific
227	  DOM node actually carries that dimension — a "reasonable-sounding" selector (the outer
228	  wrapper, the one with the semantic class name) is not guaranteed to be the right one.
229	
230	## Verification and process discipline
231	
232	- **The synthetic corpus, even a deliberately adversarial one, missed all three real bugs
233	  above.** Every one was caught only by a tester visually reviewing real, organic vault
234	  content (not synthetic fixtures, not DOM-attribute assertions). This reaffirms the
235	  postmortem's central lesson directly, in a new implementation: treat the real-vault pass
236	  as load-bearing verification for every future experiment, not a final formality after the
237	  fixtures pass.
238	- **A script-driven interaction (setting `.scrollLeft` programmatically, reading it back) is
239	  real evidence that a scroll container is *functional*, but it is not equivalent to a user
240	  actually using it.** It doesn't exercise trackpad/wheel gesture handling, doesn't reveal
241	  whether a scrollbar is visually discoverable, and doesn't catch problems that only show up
242	  under real input events. A fix that passes every computed-style and scripted-interaction
243	  check should still be handed to a tester for a final pass before being called settled if the
244	  thing being fixed is itself a user-facing interaction (as opposed to, say, a purely visual
245	  property like a color or an alignment, where a computed-style check IS the full story).
246	- **Consolidating a fix from a scratch/probe test into the "real" file is itself a step that
247	  needs re-verification, not just a mechanical copy.** A probe's injected `<style>` tag can
248	  "work" only because it's layered on top of OTHER state (here, a still-present earlier
249	  override in `styles.css`) that gets dropped when the probe's finding is folded into a single,
250	  final rule — silently losing a piece that was necessary but easy to assume the new rule
251	  subsumed. Caught only because a tester tried the rebuilt output and reported a regression,
252	  not because the "surely this is equivalent" reasoning was double-checked before shipping it.
253	- **A test that stays flaky after a plausible-looking fix may be revealing a real bug, not a
254	  residual timing issue — a fix that reduces flakiness without eliminating it hasn't
255	  necessarily fixed the actual cause.** A specific e2e test's intermittent failure was first
256	  attributed to an async DOM-settling race and "fixed" with a poll instead of a fixed sleep (a
257	  legitimate improvement in its own right, kept permanently) — but it kept failing
258	  deterministically once machine load stopped intermittently masking/unmasking the real cause.
259	  The poll-based fix was necessary but not sufficient; the actual bug (the nested-editor
260	  decoration leak, immediately above) was found only by tracing the failing assertion's own DOM
261	  ancestry down to its root, in an environment quiet enough to reproduce it every time rather
262	  than intermittently. Don't declare a flaky test "fixed" on the strength of a plausible
263	  mechanism alone — confirm the fix actually eliminates the failure, not just reduces its rate.
264	
265	- **A drastic design simplification (8 distinct shapes+colors → 1 uniform dot) is a
266	  legitimate outcome of a "build it, then actually look at it" process, not a failure of
267	  the original design.** The plan called for a distinct mark per kind specifically to test
268	  whether that was legible; building it, screenshotting it, and then judging — correctly —
269	  that the variety read as cryptic rather than helpful was the experiment doing its job, not
270	  wasted work. The per-kind version's code (quadrant/tick/bar helper functions, an 8-entry
271	  color table, a kind-keyed switch) is gone entirely from the final implementation, and the
272	  final code is smaller for it. Worth remembering when a build-and-look step contradicts an
273	  earlier plan-time assumption: revising the design mid-experiment, based on what was
274	  actually seen, is the intended use of this project's whole "confirm rather than assume,
275	  and look at the real result" discipline — not a deviation from it.

740	### What carries over from 5b
741	
742	- The **design restraint lesson**: variety without established meaning reads as noise.
743	  5a's per-kind icons passed real-content review where 5b's abstract shapes didn't, but
744	  the burden of proof stays on variety — the `markerVisibility` setting exists precisely
745	  because even good icons can be more than a leaf node needs.
746	- The **guide-through-marker-center geometry** (already independently present in 5a).
747	- **Five cross-experiment findings** (shared-value coupling, `background-position` `%`
748	  semantics, measure-the-glyph-not-the-wrapper, per-depth vs. per-line adjustments,
749	  simplification-as-legitimate-outcome) — merged into
750	  [11-decoration-lessons.md](11-decoration-lessons.md).
751	- The **live chevron measurement** approach — 5b measured the fold chevron's reach live
752	  per render; 5a hardcoded two measured constants. Porting 5b's approach is hardening
753	  item 1 below.
754	
755	### Cross-branch bug alert (important beyond this experiment)
756	
757	5a's round 4 discovered that decorations leak into Obsidian's **nested per-cell table
758	editors** (a bare word in an actively-edited cell classifies as a "paragraph" and picks up
759	indentation + a marker *inside the cell*). The fix (ViewPlugins gated on a DOM-ancestry
760	`isNestedEditor()` check) exists **only on 5a**. The latent leak affects the un-merged 5b
761	*and the upstream 2b/1 branches* (indentation alone leaks, markers just made it obvious).
762	No action needed on the chosen path — 5a carries the fix and everything merges through it
763	— but if the upstream experiment branches are ever revived independently, they need the
764	same guard.
765	
766	## Next steps: hardening 5a
767	
768	Concerns identified in the post-experiment code review (2026-07-19), ranked. None are
769	architecture-threatening; they're hardening tasks, invariants to protect, and deferred
770	polish for when 5a graduates from experiment to the real implementation.
771	
772	1. **Replace the two hardcoded fold-chevron constants with live measurement.** The chevron
773	   repositioning is a static CSS transform (`translateX(calc(-1 * (var(--to-marker-gutter)
774	   + 0.425rem - 3px)))`) where `0.425rem` is half the chevron wrapper's measured 22px width
775	   and `3px` is measured internal dead space. This is the one place 5a violates the
776	   project's own "read native values live instead of hardcoding compensations" rule
777	   ([11-decoration-lessons.md](11-decoration-lessons.md)); a theme or Obsidian update that
778	   resizes the chevron silently degrades the layout (cosmetic-only failure). 5b's session
779	   solved the same problem with live measurement — port that approach.
780	2. **Protect two documented invariants in code review.** (a) DOM injection into widget-atom
781	   subtrees works because Obsidian never re-diffs those opaque subtrees — an undocumented
782	   invariant; failure mode on an Obsidian change is re-injection flicker or duplicated
783	   markers. (b) Never append children into a plain `.cm-line` — confirmed to peg the
784	   renderer at 100%+ CPU via CM6's mutation-observer feedback loop (documented in
785	   `decorations.ts`'s module comment). Both must survive future refactors.
786	3. **Evaluate `app.workspace.updateOptions()` as a replacement for the `forceRedraw`
787	   off/on mode-toggle hack** (used to refresh `MarginCompensation` after a settings change
788	   when decoration output is byte-identical). The hack works and its reasoning is sound,
789	   but toggling a user-visible mode as an internal refresh is fragile if mode toggling
790	   ever gains side effects. `updateOptions()` is Obsidian's public API for exactly
791	   "editor-extension-affecting settings changed" — and obsidian-lapel (see the prior-art
792	   addendum below) confirms the pattern works in the wild: it swaps the registered
793	   extension array's entry in place and makes this one call.
794	4. **Consolidate to one shared `parse()`/`decorate()` pass per transaction.** Currently
795	   three ViewPlugins each re-parse the full document on every update (the 2b baseline did
796	   it twice). Same asymptotics, tripled constant; fine for normal notes, worth
797	   consolidating before testing against multi-thousand-line files. Decoration sets are
798	   also built for the whole document, not the viewport — a further (deferred) option;
799	   obsidian-lapel demonstrates the standard viewport-limited shape (build only over
800	   `view.viewport`, rebuild on `docChanged || viewportChanged`), and additionally shows
801	   that per-line *kind* facts can come from CM6's own incremental `syntaxTree` (via
802	   `lineClassNodeProp`) with no separate reparse at all — not a fit for our own tree
803	   depths (our universal-tree semantics aren't in CM6's grammar), but potentially a fit
804	   for the kind-classification part of the work.
805	5. **Resolve the `eslint-plugin-obsidianmd` violations 5a introduced.** The marker
806	   widget's inline styles trip `obsidianmd/no-static-styles-assignment` (14 errors) and
807	   `obsidianmd/prefer-create-el` — the official lint rules this project's "perfect
808	   scorecard" bar (decision Q1) commits to satisfying. Static styles should move to CSS

829	## Prior-art addendum: obsidian-lapel (2026-07-19)
830	
831	Reviewed after the head-to-head verdict, before calling the experiment wrapped:
832	[obsidian-lapel](https://github.com/liamcain/obsidian-lapel) (by Liam Cain, an Obsidian
833	team member) ships a related idea — per-level heading markers ("H1"…"H6") with a
834	click-to-change-level menu. Four source files, so the whole implementation was read.
835	
836	**Mechanism: a third approach neither 5a nor 5b tried.** Lapel renders markers in a CM6
837	`gutter()` — a dedicated column left of the content area, ordered relative to the
838	line-number gutter via extension precedence (`Prec.high`/`Prec.low`). Because a gutter
839	marker lives entirely outside `.cm-content`, *every* hard problem this experiment fought
840	simply doesn't exist there: no text-layout impact, no gutter reservation, no
841	`contain: paint`, no fold-chevron collision, no blockquote `::before` conflict, no
842	readable-line-width margin interaction. The trade-off is structural: a gutter is a flat,
843	fixed column — it cannot place a marker at the node's own indent depth, which is the whole
844	point of our design (the marker as a crown on the guide line, at the node's own column).
845	So it doesn't replace 5a's mechanism for us, but it is the right tool for flat per-line
846	chrome, worth remembering if we ever add any (e.g. node handles, diagnostics).
847	
848	**Independent confirmation of the nested-editor leak (5a's round 4).** Lapel hit the exact
849	same bug class: its gutter renders inside Obsidian's per-table-cell nested editors. Their
850	fix is a CSS hide (`.table-cell-wrapper .cm-gutters { display: none }`) with a comment
851	calling it a hack to remove "once there's a proper way to not register the editor
852	extension inside table cells" — i.e. they too found no official API to scope
853	`registerEditorExtension`, and their workaround only hides the symptom (the extension
854	still runs). Our DOM-ancestry `isNestedEditor()` gate is structurally stronger: it stops
855	the computation, not just the paint.
856	
857	**Confirmations picked up into the hardening checklist** (items 3 and 4 above):
858	`app.workspace.updateOptions()` as the settings-refresh mechanism, viewport-limited
859	building, and CM6's own `syntaxTree` as a kind-classification source.
860	
861	### Potential follow-ups (deliberately out of Experiment 5's scope)
862	
863	- **Per-level heading markers (H1–H6).** Considered during the experiment but kept out to
864	  avoid scope creep; lapel validates the idea in the wild. Under 5a's mechanism this is
865	  small: carry the heading `level` (already on `OutlineNode`, present iff
866	  `kind === 'heading'`) through `LineDecorationFact`, and either branch
867	  `buildMarkerIcon` per level or render a small text label. Lapel's customization
868	  pattern is worth copying regardless of the visual: a `data-level` attribute plus a
869	  CSS-custom-property indirection (`--heading-marker`, consumed by `content:`) lets
870	  themes/snippets restyle markers per level without touching the plugin — our markers
871	  could expose `data-kind` (and `data-level`) the same way.
872	- **Marker interactivity.** Lapel's markers are clickable: a `Menu` listing heading
873	  levels 1–6 (checked state on the current level, `lucide-heading-N` icons) plus a
874	  "Body" option, dispatching a line rewrite. This is exactly the "future interactivity"
875	  potential the head-to-head credited 5a's real-DOM mechanism with — a node's marker as
876	  a click target for outline operations (change kind/level, fold, zoom, structural
877	  moves). Two caveats from lapel's own code: our `MarkerWidget` currently sets
878	  `pointer-events: none` + `ignoreEvent() { return true }`, both of which would need
879	  revisiting carefully (CM6 widget event handling interacts with editor focus/cursor);
880	  and lapel positions its menu via `Menu.setParentElement`, which is NOT public API
881	  (they augment `obsidian.d.ts` locally) — a public-API-only equivalent
882	  (`showAtMouseEvent` alone) needs verifying against our bar first.
883	
884	## Open question: shrinking only our own added list margin
885	
886	Raised during Experiment 1's review, not yet decided. The deferred list-hang issue (see [Experiment 1's results](08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md)) is native
887	Obsidian chrome and explicitly out of scope for direct edits in this experiment. But a
888	narrower variant stays inside the additive-only discipline: **reduce only the margin *we*
889	add** to list items — not native `text-indent`/`padding-left` — by the list's own native
890	hang width, read live via `getComputedStyle` the same way Experiment 1's table fix (bug #3 in
891	[08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md))
892	reads and compensates for native padding. Worth exploring as a follow-up, with two open
893	risks to resolve before trying it: (a) clamping so a shallow `supplementalDepth` never goes
894	negative once the hang is subtracted (the table fix's `max(0px, ...)` pattern applies
895	directly), and (b) the compensation must be based on the list **root**'s own hang, not each
896	item's — nested items can have wider markers (e.g. `10.` vs `-`) with different native hang
897	widths, and compensating per-item instead of per-root-chain would reintroduce exactly the
898	kind of within-list misalignment the wide-numbering fixture exists to catch.

1	/**
2	 * CM6 adapter for outline mode's decorations. All the pure computation
3	 * (depth/supplementalDepth, per-line guide depths) lives in decorate.ts;
4	 * this module only turns those facts into CM6 decorations/DOM, gated
5	 * per-editor on outline mode via the public `editorInfoField` — same
6	 * gating pattern as keymap.ts's grammarExtension.
7	 *
8	 * Two mechanisms, because Obsidian renders atom kinds two different ways in
9	 * Live Preview:
10	 *
11	 * - Most lines (headings, paragraphs, list items, code fences, plain
12	 *   blockquotes) render as a real `.cm-line` that CM6 lets us decorate
13	 *   declaratively: one class plus one CSS custom property per fact (never
14	 *   an inline shorthand property), so styles.css owns the actual
15	 *   `padding-left`/`margin-left` rules and their units (additive-only
16	 *   indentation, Experiment 1) plus the guide-line gradient (Experiment 2b,
17	 *   see the "Guide lines" section below), PLUS a real CM6 `Decoration.widget`
18	 *   for the block marker icon (Experiment 5a, see below) — CM6's own,
19	 *   supported way to insert extra DOM into a line's content without
20	 *   fighting its own re-render/diffing.
21	 * - Tables, callouts, raw HTML blocks, and horizontal rules are rendered as
22	 *   opaque replacement widgets (`.cm-embed-block`, or `.hr` for the rule) —
23	 *   confirmed live: a `Decoration.line` targeting that line's position has
24	 *   no effect at all (not even a class-merge partial win), because the
25	 *   widget's own `toDOM()` produces the line's DOM wholesale and neither
26	 *   CM6 nor Obsidian threads our decoration's class/attributes through it.
27	 *   These need a direct, imperative DOM patch instead — a `ViewPlugin`
28	 *   that, after each render, sets `margin-left` inline (with `!important`,
29	 *   which always wins for an inline style regardless of what any
30	 *   stylesheet rule does), and appends a marker icon child, on whichever
31	 *   such widgets are currently mounted. Appending a child directly into one
32	 *   of these widgets is safe (proven across this experiment's e2e runs):
33	 *   they're opaque, Obsidian-owned subtrees CM6 never re-diffs internally.
34	 *   The equivalent is NOT safe for a plain `.cm-line`, which CM6 actively
35	 *   owns/re-renders — an earlier version of this code tried appending a
36	 *   marker child directly into `.cm-line`s from this same ViewPlugin (to
37	 *   get live-measured multi-line height for code fences) and it pegged
38	 *   Obsidian's renderer at 100%+ CPU indefinitely, almost certainly CM6's
39	 *   own DOM-mutation observer (used to detect external/IME edits) reacting
40	 *   to the unexpected child and re-triggering updates in a feedback loop.
41	 *   Reverted; plain-line markers stay on the CM6-native `Decoration.widget`
42	 *   path below, which has no such risk.
43	 *
44	 * `MarginCompensation` (below) additionally patches margin-left on BOTH
45	 * mechanisms to account for Obsidian's own "readable line width" feature,
46	 * which applies a `margin-inline: auto`-centering rule to every `.cm-line`
47	 * (any div child of `.cm-content`, in fact) — a *uniform* native base
48	 * margin our own `calc(depth * unit) !important` rule was silently
49	 * *replacing* instead of adding to, confirmed live (a depth-0 heading and
50	 * a depth-1 list item under it rendered with the list-item's own box to
51	 * the LEFT of the heading's, an inverted/negative-looking indentation —
52	 * reported by real-vault testing under a community theme with a narrower
53	 * reading column than the bundled themes, where the effect became large
54	 * enough to notice; the bug itself is present under any theme/viewport
55	 * where that base margin is nonzero, bundled themes included). See its
56	 * own doc comment below for the fix.
57	 */

79	// ---- Guide lines (Experiment 2b: CSS stacked-gradient) ---------------------
80	//
81	// @replit/codemirror-indentation-markers' technique: one `--to-guides`
82	// custom property per line, a comma-joined list of repeating-linear-
83	// gradient layers (one per active ancestor depth), consumed by a single
84	// `::before` — O(1) DOM nodes regardless of depth, no JS pixel measurement,
85	// no overlay layer (contrast Experiment 2a's measured overlay divs).
86	//
87	// `to-decor-atom`/`to-decor-list` lines use `margin-left` (Experiment 1's
88	// fix for the "padding never moves the box" bug), which DOES shift the
89	// line's own box — an earlier version of this code concluded from that
90	// alone that a guide could never render on those lines, since their own
91	// `::before` (position: absolute, relative to the line's own box) can't
92	// reach a shallower ancestor's column. That reasoning had a real bug: it
93	// assumed a background is clipped to the *positioned element's own box*,
94	// but the pseudo-element's own box does NOT have to match its containing
95	// block's dimensions — `left`/`right` can widen it arbitrarily, including
96	// leftward past where the line's box starts. Confirmed live (screenshot +
97	// computed style) that nothing in the ancestor chain up to `.cm-scroller`
98	// clips that overflow (`.cm-content`/`.cm-contentContainer`/`.cm-sizer`
99	// are all `overflow: visible`; `.cm-scroller` is `overflow: auto` but its
100	// own box starts well to the left of any guide column we'd ever need).
101	//
102	// The needed compensation ("how far has this line's own box been shifted
103	// right of the global column origin") is fully known at decoration-build
104	// time for both kinds, with NO live measurement: an atom's own shift is
105	// exactly `depth * unit` (our own margin-left value). A list item's own
106	// shift is exactly `supplementalDepth * unit` (our own margin-left value)
107	// too — confirmed live that Obsidian's native hang (`text-indent`/
108	// `padding-left`, applied to the very same `.cm-line`) contributes NOTHING
109	// to the box's own position: `getBoundingClientRect()` on a list line
110	// showed its box's left edge exactly matching its `margin-left`, despite a
111	// nonzero native `padding-left`/`text-indent` also being present — because
112	// neither property moves a box's own edges (padding shifts content only;
113	// text-indent shifts only the first inline line's content, per the CSS
114	// spec), only `margin` does. So both kinds can widen their own pseudo's
115	// box by exactly `--to-own-shift` units to reach any shallower ancestor's
116	// column, with no measurement beyond the JS constants this module already
117	// computes.
118	const UNIT = 'var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)';
119	
120	function guideLayer(depth: number): string {
121	  return (
122	    `repeating-linear-gradient(to right, var(--text-faint) 0 1px, transparent 1px ${UNIT}) ` +
123	    `calc(${depth} * ${UNIT}) 0 / ${UNIT} 100% no-repeat`
124	  );
125	}
126	
127	function guideBackground(guideDepths: readonly number[]): string {
128	  return guideDepths.map(guideLayer).join(', ');
129	}

134	// small, distinct, self-drawn SVG icon per node kind, rendered on a node's
135	// own first line only (never a list item — the native bullet/number already

141	// icon's own left edge at the column, and no gutter with the icon's own
142	// right edge at the column — read worse in a real vault and were dropped).
143	// `MarkerVisibility` itself lives in mode-registry.ts (not here), so that
144	// pure, Obsidian-free module can keep defining PluginData without importing
145	// this one (which pulls in the real `obsidian` package for
146	// `editorInfoField`).
147	export type { MarkerVisibility } from './mode-registry';
148	import type { MarkerVisibility } from './mode-registry';

150	/** Anything that can supply decorations needs to say which notes are in

152	 * Obsidian setting, read fresh on every recompute so switching it live (no
153	 * rebuild) takes effect on the very next transaction, the same way toggling
154	 * outline mode already does (see main.ts's refreshDecorations). */
155	export interface DecorationSource extends ModeSource {

159	/**
160	 * Whether a given node's marker should render at all (Experiment 5a
161	 * follow-up — see `MarkerVisibility`'s own doc comment in mode-registry.ts
162	 * for the reasoning). Deliberately does NOT touch the marker gutter
163	 * reservation (padding-left/margin-left) at all — that stays reserved
164	 * uniformly regardless of this setting, so hiding some markers never
165	 * reflows text/shifts indentation; only whether the icon itself is drawn
166	 * in that already-reserved space changes.
167	 */
168	function shouldShowMarker(fact: LineDecorationFact, visibility: MarkerVisibility): boolean {
169	  switch (visibility) {
170	    case 'all':
171	      return true;
172	    case 'with-children':
173	      return fact.hasChildren;
174	    case 'headings-and-paragraphs':
175	      // The only two marker-eligible kinds that can ever have children in
176	      // this tree model — atoms are leaves by construction (see hasChildren
177	      // itself), so `!fact.isAtom` is exactly "heading or paragraph."
178	      return !fact.isAtom;
179	  }
180	}

182	const MARKER_GUTTER_REM = 1.25;
183	const MARKER_ICON_REM = 0.85;
184	// The actual CSS length emitted per line (see lineDecoration()) — a single
185	// source of truth the static CSS rules, the live margin overrides, AND the
186	// marker's own left-offset calc all agree with. Every non-list-item line
187	// reserves this gutter unconditionally (see lineDecoration()'s own
188	// reasoning for why list items don't).
189	const MARKER_GUTTER_CSS = `${MARKER_GUTTER_REM}rem`;
190	const MARKER_ICON_CSS = `${MARKER_ICON_REM}rem`;
191	
192	/**
193	 * Where a marker icon's own LEFT edge should sit, given `targetRelExpr` — a
194	 * CSS length expression for "the shared target column (where the guide for
195	 * this depth renders), relative to the box the marker is about to become a
196	 * child of" (see call sites: block/atom/widget-atom each derive this from
197	 * their own already-established `--to-own-shift`-style formula, so the
198	 * marker automatically stays correct if those formulas ever change). Used
199	 * only by the widget-atom mechanism below (table/callout/hr/html) — the
200	 * plain-line mechanism uses `MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR` instead (see its own
201	 * doc comment for why the two need different math). Centers the icon on the
202	 * target column (the placement exploration's winner — see the module doc
203	 * comment above).
204	 */
205	function markerAnchorLeftExpr(targetRelExpr: string): string {
206	  return `calc(${targetRelExpr} - (${MARKER_ICON_CSS} / 2))`;
207	}

209	/**

211	 * see `MarkerWidget`'s own doc comment for why this uses a fundamentally
212	 * different technique (inline + `vertical-align`, not `position: absolute`
213	 * relative to the line's own box) from the widget-atom mechanism above.
214	 *
215	 * Because the widget is always inserted at the exact position where the
216	 * node's own text starts (CM6 `Decoration.widget` at the line's first
217	 * character, `side: -1`), and — by construction — that text position is
218	 * always exactly `gutter` to the right of the shared target column,
219	 * regardless of kind or depth (that IS the definition of the gutter), the
220	 * needed shift collapses to a single depth/kind-independent expression:
221	 * `iconSize * 0.5 - gutter` (icon centered on the column). Worked through
222	 * concretely for both block (padding-shifted text, unshifted box) and
223	 * atom-plain (margin-shifted box, unshifted-relative-to-box text) — the
224	 * depth terms cancel identically in both cases, confirmed by hand before
225	 * relying on it here (see the git history of this comment for the full
226	 * derivation).
227	 */
228	const MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR = `calc(${MARKER_ICON_CSS} * 0.5 - ${MARKER_GUTTER_CSS})`;

232	function svgEl<K extends keyof SVGElementTagNameMap>(
233	  tag: K,
234	  attrs: Record<string, string>,
235	): SVGElementTagNameMap[K] {
236	  const el = document.createElementNS(SVG_NS, tag);
237	  for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(attrs)) el.setAttribute(key, value);
238	  return el;
239	}

243	  'stroke-width': '1.5',

257	  const svg = svgEl('svg', { viewBox: '0 0 16 16', width: '100%', height: '100%' });

268	    case 'paragraph':

271	        svgEl('line', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x1: '2', y1: '4', x2: '14', y2: '4' }),
272	        svgEl('line', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x1: '2', y1: '8', x2: '14', y2: '8' }),
273	        svgEl('line', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x1: '2', y1: '12', x2: '9', y2: '12' }),
274	      );
275	      break;
276	    case 'code':
277	      // "</>"
278	      svg.append(
279	        svgEl('polyline', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, points: '6,3 2,8 6,13' }),
280	        svgEl('line', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x1: '9.5', y1: '2', x2: '6.5', y2: '14' }),
281	        svgEl('polyline', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, points: '10,3 14,8 10,13' }),
282	      );
283	      break;
284	    case 'table':
285	      // 2x2 grid.
286	      svg.append(
287	        svgEl('rect', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x: '2', y: '2', width: '12', height: '12', rx: '1' }),
288	        svgEl('line', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x1: '2', y1: '8', x2: '14', y2: '8' }),
289	        svgEl('line', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x1: '8', y1: '2', x2: '8', y2: '14' }),
290	      );
291	      break;
292	    case 'callout':
293	      // Filled alert circle with an "!" bar.
294	      svg.append(
295	        svgEl('circle', { cx: '8', cy: '8', r: '6', fill: 'currentColor' }),
296	        svgEl('rect', { x: '7', y: '4', width: '2', height: '5', fill: 'var(--background-primary)' }),
297	        svgEl('rect', { x: '7', y: '10', width: '2', height: '2', fill: 'var(--background-primary)' }),
298	      );
299	      break;
300	    case 'quote':
301	      // Two opening-quote marks.
302	      svg.append(
303	        svgEl('circle', { cx: '5', cy: '5', r: '2', fill: 'currentColor' }),
304	        svgEl('rect', { x: '4', y: '5', width: '2', height: '4', fill: 'currentColor' }),
305	        svgEl('circle', { cx: '11', cy: '5', r: '2', fill: 'currentColor' }),
306	        svgEl('rect', { x: '10', y: '5', width: '2', height: '4', fill: 'currentColor' }),
307	      );
308	      break;
309	    case 'html':
310	      // An outlined tag/document shape with a folded corner.
311	      svg.append(
312	        svgEl('rect', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x: '3', y: '2', width: '10', height: '12', rx: '1' }),
313	        svgEl('line', { ...STROKE_ATTRS, x1: '9', y1: '2', x2: '13', y2: '6' }),
314	      );
315	      break;
316	    case 'hr':
317	      // A single bold horizontal bar.

320	    default:
321	      // Unreachable for list-item (excluded by every caller) — a small dot

323	      svg.append(svgEl('circle', { cx: '8', cy: '8', r: '2', fill: 'currentColor' }));
324	  }

326	  return svg;

334	/**

338	 * its own doc comment for why. A widget atom IS its own single, opaque,
339	 * already-full-height DOM element (unlike a `.cm-line`, which can carry

419	function computeMarkers(state: EditorState, modes: DecorationSource): DecorationSet {

421	  if (!path || !modes.isOutline(path)) return Decoration.none;

423	  const doc = parse(state.doc.toString());

428	    // guides already use); continuation lines never repeat the marker.

431	    // to attach to — MarginCompensation injects their marker directly.
432	    if (WIDGET_ATOM_KINDS.has(fact.kind)) continue;
433	    if (!shouldShowMarker(fact, modes.markerVisibility)) continue;
434	    if (fact.lineNumber >= totalLines) continue; // stale fact past a shrunk doc
435	
436	    const from = state.doc.line(fact.lineNumber + 1).from; // CM6 lines are 1-indexed
437	    builder.add(
438	      from,
439	      from,
440	      Decoration.widget({ widget: new MarkerWidget(fact.kind, MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR), side: -1 }),

446	function lineDecoration(fact: LineDecorationFact, guide: LineGuideFact): Decoration {

450	  // gutter where applicable) this line's own box has been shifted right by
451	  // its own margin-left — the exact compensation the guide's pseudo needs
452	  // to widen its box by, leftward, to reach a shallower ancestor's column
453	  // (see the doc comment above `guideLayer`). `undefined` means the box
454	  // isn't shifted at all (block lines: padding-left never moves the box).
455	  let ownShiftExpr: string | undefined;
456	
457	  if (fact.isListItem) {
458	    cls = 'to-decor-list';
459	    styles.push(`--to-supp-depth: ${fact.supplementalDepth}`);
460	    // List items get no marker gutter (native bullet/number only).
461	    ownShiftExpr = fact.supplementalDepth > 0 ? `calc(${fact.supplementalDepth} * ${UNIT})` : undefined;
462	  } else if (fact.isAtom) {
463	    cls = 'to-decor-atom';
464	    styles.push(`--to-depth: ${fact.depth}`);
465	    styles.push(`--to-marker-gutter: ${MARKER_GUTTER_CSS}`);
466	    // Every non-list line reserves the marker gutter, so the box is always
467	    // shifted by at least the gutter, even at depth 0.
468	    ownShiftExpr = `calc(${fact.depth} * ${UNIT} + var(--to-marker-gutter, 0px))`;

479	    if (ownShiftExpr) {
480	      styles.push(`--to-own-shift: ${ownShiftExpr}`);

498	function computeDecorations(state: EditorState, modes: DecorationSource): DecorationSet {
499	  const path = state.field(editorInfoField, false)?.file?.path;
500	  if (!path || !modes.isOutline(path)) return Decoration.none;
501	
502	  const doc = parse(state.doc.toString());
503	  // computeLineGuides is a strict superset of decorate() by line coverage
504	  // (every line decorate() covers, plus gap-only lines) — iterate it as
505	  // the primary sequence (still ascending by lineNumber, required by
506	  // RangeSetBuilder) and look up the matching decorate() fact by line
507	  // number instead of assuming index alignment, since gap lines have no
508	  // corresponding entry there at all.
509	  const factsByLine = new Map(decorate(doc).map((f) => [f.lineNumber, f]));
510	  const guides = computeLineGuides(doc);
511	  const totalLines = state.doc.lines;
512	  const builder = new RangeSetBuilder<Decoration>();
513	  for (const guide of guides) {
514	    if (guide.lineNumber >= totalLines) continue; // stale fact past a shrunk doc
515	    const from = state.doc.line(guide.lineNumber + 1).from; // CM6 lines are 1-indexed

517	      if (guide.guideDepths.length === 0) continue; // nothing to draw

520	    }
521	    const fact = factsByLine.get(guide.lineNumber);
522	    if (!fact) continue; // decorate()/computeLineGuides walks are in sync; defensive only

664	  /**

683	    // previous `apply()` call already set its margin-left, querying it

770	    // Plain lines (atoms/list items rendered as genuine `.cm-line`s, not

823	        const nativeShift =
824	          (parseFloat(iconLineStyle.paddingLeft) || 0) + (parseFloat(iconLineStyle.textIndent) || 0);
825	        // Always SET (never remove) `left` here: this element is the SAME
826	        // node `MarkerWidget.toDOM()` already applied its own base
827	        // `left` to (in the same inline-style object) — `removeProperty`
828	        // would delete that value entirely (falling back to `auto`, i.e.
829	        // no shift at all) instead of restoring it, a real bug caught live
830	        // when a blockquote's own padding/text-indent pair summed to
831	        // exactly 0 and the marker landed with no shift whatsoever.
832	        icon.style.setProperty(
833	          'left',
834	          nativeShift !== 0
835	            ? `calc(${MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR} - ${nativeShift}px)`
836	            : MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR,
837	          'important',
838	        );
839	      }
840	    }
841	  }
842	
843	  private clearAll(): void {
844	    const widgets = Array.from(
845	      this.view.contentDOM.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(WIDGET_ATOM_SELECTOR),
846	    );
847	    for (const el of widgets) {
848	      el.style.removeProperty('margin-left');
849	      el.classList.remove('to-decor-guides');
850	      el.style.removeProperty('--to-guides');
851	      el.style.removeProperty('--to-own-shift');
852	      clearWidgetMarker(el);
853	    }
854	    const plainLines = Array.from(
855	      this.view.contentDOM.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(PLAIN_MARGIN_SELECTOR),
856	    );
857	    for (const el of plainLines) el.style.removeProperty('margin-left');
858	  }
859	}
860	
861	export function decorationsExtension(modes: DecorationSource): Extension {
862	  return [
863	    // ViewPlugins (not plain StateFields) specifically so each has `view`
864	    // access to run isNestedEditor's DOM-ancestry check — state alone can't
865	    // tell a nested per-cell editor apart from the real top-level note (see
866	    // isNestedEditor's own doc comment). Recomputes on every update, not
867	    // just docChanged ones: toggling outline mode has no doc change of its
868	    // own, only a nudged selection transaction (see main.ts) to make these
869	    // re-run.
870	    ViewPlugin.define((view) => new DecorationsPlugin(view, modes), {
871	      decorations: (v) => v.decorations,
872	    }),
873	    // A SEPARATE plugin for block-marker widgets (Experiment 5a), not
874	    // merged into the same RangeSetBuilder as the line decorations above —
875	    // CM6 merges decorations from multiple sources correctly on its own,
876	    // sidestepping any need to reason about Decoration.line/Decoration.
877	    // widget ordering at the same document position.
878	    ViewPlugin.define((view) => new MarkersPlugin(view, modes), {
879	      decorations: (v) => v.decorations,
880	    }),
881	    ViewPlugin.define<MarginCompensation>((view) => new MarginCompensation(view, modes)),
882	  ];
883	}
